data: {"architecture_name": "Deep-MLP Token Mixer (D-MTM)", "core_mechanism": "Deep Residual MLP Blocks without Self-Attention", "layer_stack": "Embedding + Positional Encoding -> [Norm -> Deep MLP Stack x 24]", "connection_pattern": "Residual Skip Connections", "training_approach": "Pretraining on long-context corpora", "hypothesis_rationale": "Leverages the theoretical finding that sufficient depth in MLPs can approximate attention dependencies, avoiding quadratic complexity."}

Leverages the theoretical finding that sufficient depth in MLPs can approximate attention dependencies — The hypothesis that deep MLP stacks can approximate attention dependencies contradicts the consensus that MLPs lack the explicit global connectivity required for efficient long-range dependency modeling. Empirical results on long-context benchmarks demonstrate that MLPs suffer from a **global mixing bottleneck**, where information propagation degrades over depth, preventing the model from accessing global context effectively. This architectural limitation necessitates exponentially greater depth to capture long-range correlations that attention handles linearly, leading to optimization instability and performance collapse on tasks requiring consistent long-term memory. Consequently, the proposed architecture is theoretically unsound for long-context tasks where information fidelity is paramount.

{"architecture_name": "Hybrid Local-Global Mixer (H-LGM)", "changes_made": "Introduced Linear Attention module to address global mixing bottleneck in pure MLPs", "layer_stack": "Embedding -> [Norm -> Linear Attention (Global)] -> [Norm -> Deep MLP Stack x 12] -> [Residual]", "connection_pattern": "Hybrid Residual (Local MLP + Global Linear Attention)", "training_approach": "Contrastive Pretraining on Long-Context Corpora + SFT on Reasoning Tasks"}

The proposal's claim that Linear Attention adequately addresses the global mixing bottleneck. — The claim that Linear Attention resolves the global mixing bottleneck contradicts the consensus that approximate kernels inherently discard high-frequency information, which is critical for capturing complex token correlations. Empirical studies show that these approximations lead to measurable performance degradation on reasoning tasks compared to exact Self-Attention, even when accounting for computational efficiency. Consequently, this substitution risks introducing a fundamental bottleneck that undermines the reasoning capabilities required for the specified SFT training regimen.

{"architecture_name": "Sparse-MLP Hybrid Mixer (SMHM)", "changes_made": "Replaced Linear Attention with Sparse Attention (Sparse Attention Pattern) + Deep MLP for local token mixing", "layer_stack": "Embedding + RoPE Positional Encoding -> [Norm -> Sparse Attention (Log-Sparse)] -> [Norm -> Deep MLP Stack x 16] -> [Norm -> Sparse Attention (Log-Sparse)]", "connection_pattern": "Alternating Sparse Attention + Deep MLP with Skip Connections", "training_approach": "Mixture of Token Attention + Contrastive Pretraining on Long-Context Data + RLHF for Reasoning"}

Deep MLPs compensate for Sparse Attention limitations — The claim that Deep MLPs can sufficiently compensate for the expressive limitations of static Log-Sparse Attention contradicts the established consensus that global coherence in language modeling requires full-rank attention mechanisms to prevent long-range dependency collapse. Empirical evidence from long-context benchmarks (e.g., LongBench) demonstrates that static sparse patterns induce a severe information bottleneck, causing a significant degradation in retrieval accuracy for tokens separated by large distances. Since Deep MLPs are inherently local and lack the non-linear weighting to bridge these gaps, this design is prone to catastrophic forgetting of early context, a failure mode that persists regardless of MLP depth or contrastive pretraining objectives.

{"architecture_name": "Efficient Full-Rank Attention Mixer (E-FRAM)", "changes_made": "Replaced Sparse Attention with Flash Attention 2 + Grouped Query Attention (GQA) for full-rank global coherence while maintaining computational efficiency", "layer_stack": "Embedding + RoPE Positional Encoding -> [Flash Attention 2 + GQA x 12] -> [Norm -> Deep MLP Stack x 8] -> [Norm -> Flash Attention 2 + GQA x 12] -> [Norm -> Deep MLP Stack x 8]", "connection_pattern": "Alternating Full-Rank Attention (via GQA) + Deep MLP with Residual Skip Connections", "training_approach": "Flash Attention Optimized Pretraining + Contrastive Long-Context Pretraining + SFT on Reasoning Tasks"}

The claim that Flash Attention 2 + GQA maintains computational efficiency for full-rank global coherence — Flash Attention 2 optimizes memory bandwidth but does not reduce the fundamental $O(N^2)$ computational complexity of full-rank attention, directly contradicting the claim of maintaining efficiency for long contexts. While GQA reduces KV cache memory, it does not alter the quadratic scaling of Q*K computations, meaning the proposed architecture inherits the same memory bottlenecks as standard dense attention that fail beyond standard context windows, ignoring empirical evidence that quadratic memory growth inevitably leads to OOM and training instability. Consequently, replacing sparse mechanisms with dense attention disregards the theoretical necessity of sub-quadratic attention for scaling beyond 128k tokens, rendering the proposed architecture theoretically unsound for the claimed long-context capabilities.

{"architecture_name": "Hybrid SSM-MLP Mixer (H-SMM)", "changes_made": "Replaced Flash Attention with State Space Model (SSM) modules (Mamba/JAXSSM) for O(N) global context processing, combined with Deep MLP for non-linear local mixing", "layer_stack": "Embedding + RoPE Positional Encoding -> [SSM Block x 16] -> [Norm -> Deep MLP Stack x 8] -> [SSM Block x 16] -> [Norm -> Deep MLP Stack x 8]", "connection_pattern": "Alternating SSM (Linear Global) + Deep MLP (Local Non-Linear) with Residual Skip Connections", "training_approach": "Sequential Pretraining on Long-Context Corpora + Contrastive Long-Context Fine-Tuning + RLHF for Reasoning"}

{"architecture_name": "Hybrid SSM-MLP-Attention Mixer (H-SAMM)", "full_specification": "Layer Stack: [Embedding + RoPE Positional Encoding] -> [Norm -> SSM Block (O(N) Global) x 8] -> [Norm -> Deep MLP Stack (Local Non-Linear) x 4] -> [Norm -> Hybrid Attention Block (Sparse+Dense) x 8] -> [Norm -> SSM Block x 8] -> [Norm -> Deep MLP x 4] -> [Output Projection]", "design_rationale": "Combines SSM for linear global coherence, MLP for local non-linear mixing, and hybrid attention blocks to address information bottleneck concerns while maintaining O(N) complexity", "predicted_advantages": "Sub-quadratic memory scaling, full-rank global coherence, reasoning capability preservation, and computational efficiency for contexts beyond 128k tokens", "open_question": "1. Optimal ratio of SSM:MLP:Hybrid-Attention blocks for different task types; 2. How hybrid attention bridges frequency information loss without incurring quadratic cost; 3. Training stability when mixing different module types; 4. Generalization across domain-specific long-context tasks"}
